Former Davidson Fabric Factory. Large deteriorating multi-story brick industrial building with peeling paint and green shuttered windows. Long-vacant manufacturing facility. Coordinates corrected Apr 2026.
Chain-link fenced lot with deteriorating blue/teal commercial building visible in Nov 2020 SV. Demolition reportedly started June 2025. Site awaiting redevelopment. Coordinates corrected Apr 2026.

English Station power plant. Former United Illuminating coal-fired power plant, decommissioned 1992. Smokestacks visible behind graffiti-covered concrete perimeter wall. Fenced off, no activity. Environmental contamination site. One of New Havens most prominent abandoned industrial landmarks. Coordinates corrected Apr 2026.

Former Landover Mall. 87-acre site demolished 2006. Mar 2022 SV shows vast empty parking lot with chain-link fencing. Proposed $5B data center project (Brightseat Tech Park) announced Oct 2024 but paused due to public opposition. Site has been vacant lot for 20 years. Adjacent to FedExField. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Potomac River Generating Station. Former coal-fired power plant built 1948, decommissioned 2012. 20-acre waterfront site. Purchased by HRP Group 2020. Demolition planned 2027, mixed-use redevelopment with 2000 residential units by 2029-2030. Environmental remediation ongoing. No street-level SV coverage (fenced site). Old Town North. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Downtown Welch commercial block. Mar 2025 SV shows multiple boarded storefronts with deteriorating aluminum siding and cardboard/plywood over windows. McDowell County seat, population declined from 6600 (1950) to under 2000. Coal economy collapse. Link corrected Apr 2026.
Downtown Welch. Mar 2025 SV shows large empty storefront with glass windows revealing completely vacant interior. Deteriorating brick building on main commercial street. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Downtown Welch. Mar 2025 SV shows multiple multi-story brick commercial buildings with deteriorating exteriors, debris in parking area. Adjacent to active WIC office at 28 McDowell, but most commercial buildings on block appear underused or vacant. Link corrected Apr 2026.

McLure Hotel. Historic hotel built 1852, operated as Ramada/OYO. Condemned Nov 2024 by City of Wheeling. Code violations: mold, fire safety, sewage, electrical. Tenants evicted. Health dept permits suspended indefinitely. Prospective buyer from Arkansas in negotiations as of Dec 2024. 9-story landmark building on Market St. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Former Wheeling-Pittsburgh Steel headquarters. 12-story office building vacant since RG Steel bankruptcy in 2012. Sep 2025 SV shows building behind iron fencing. Announced conversion to 128 market-rate apartments but renovation timeline uncertain. Downtown Wheeling landmark. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Former 400 block of Federal St. Multiple commercial buildings including 8-story Montgomery Ward demolished Feb-Jul 2024 by city. Jun 2024 SV shows active demolition with excavator. Site is now cleared lot awaiting redevelopment as community park/green space. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Downtown Bluefield commercial corridor. Jun 2024 SV shows closed storefronts with green roll-down gate and darkened windows facing Scott St. American flag and flower basket on light post but no signs of active business. Adjacent brick building also appears vacant. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Downtown Cairo, Commercial Ave. Multi-story brick commercial building, most prominent surviving structure on Commercial Ave. Dark windows, deteriorating facade, green-painted ground floor storefront. Downtown Cairo, Commercial Ave. Row of closed storefronts with dark/shuttered windows. No signs of active business. Nov 2025 SV. Empty demolished lots across the street where other Commercial Ave businesses once stood. Link corrected Apr 2026.

address: 417 Missouri Ave, East St Louis, IL 62201
notes: Spivey Building. Only skyscraper ever built in East St. Louis. 12-story Commercial-style building constructed 1927 by newspaper owner Allen Spivey, designed by Albert B. Frankel. Abandoned since ~1980. May 2021 SV shows "SPIVEY" engraved entrance, chain-link fencing, severely deteriorating brick facade, missing/broken windows. No roof, gutted interior. National Register of Historic Places. Named 2025 Most Endangered Historic Place in Illinois by Landmarks Illinois. Part of Downtown East St. Louis Historic District. Link corrected Apr 2026.

address: 240 Collinsville Ave, East St Louis, IL 62201
notes: Majestic Theater. Grand theater opened Feb 26, 1928, featuring Pewabic tile mosaics and Wurlitzer organ (organ later moved to Fox Theatre in St. Louis). Closed 1960. Apr 2025 SV shows ornate facade still standing amid downtown blight. Adjacent Murphy Building (234 Collinsville) being demolished 2024-2025. Part of Downtown East St. Louis Historic District (National Register). Link corrected Apr 2026.

AT&T Tower (formerly One SBC Center). 44-story, 1,400,000 sq ft office building built 1986 as Southwestern Bell HQ. Second-largest vacant office building in the United States. Vacant since AT&T moved all employees out in 2017. Value declined 95% from $204.9M (2006) to $9.2M. Sold at auction to Goldman Group of Boston. Apr 2025 SV shows massive brutalist tower, no activity. Redevelopment plans stalled after state tax incentives died in legislature. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Railway Exchange Building. Historic 22-story Beaux-Arts office building built 1914. Former flagship Famous-Barr department store. Vacant since 2014. City acquired via eminent domain, appraised at $4.75M. City approved $78K for security in Oct 2025 as redevelopment plans stalled. Oct 2020 SV shows ornate terra cotta facade intact. Full city block bounded by Olive, Locust, 6th, 7th streets. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Chemical Building. 17-story historic office building, National Register of Historic Places. Long vacant with missing windows, moisture damage, deteriorating brick. May 2025 SV shows upper floors boarded/covered. Listed for sale at $12M after purchase for $5M. Proposed $82M hotel conversion (240-room dual-branded Marriott) fell through. Ground floor may have retail tenant but upper floors empty. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Rockefeller Building. Historic 15-story Gilded Age office building completed 1905. Abandoned, boarded, extensively vandalized. Marble walls spray-painted, ornate brass furnishings stolen, pipes stripped causing basement flooding, windows broken. Prior owner defaulted; BridgeInvest (Miami) took over Jul 2025. K&D Group under contract to acquire as of Mar 2026. Listed as permanently closed on Google Maps. Limited street-level SV coverage due to downtown construction. Link corrected Apr 2026.

address: 8 N B.B. King Blvd, Memphis, TN 38103
notes: Sterick Building. 31-story Gothic Revival office tower, 365 ft tall. Built 1929, formerly the tallest building in the South. Vacant since 1986 — nearly 40 years. Sep 2025 SV shows darkened windows, no activity. Purchased by Constellation Properties Mar 2023 with plans for housing conversion. Received $300K TN Historic Development Grant Apr 2024 but no construction started. Named after original owners Sterling and Hedrick. Link corrected Apr 2026.

JEA Tower (former Independent Life Building). 19-story, 347,811 sq ft office building built 1955. Tallest abandoned building in downtown Jacksonville for almost 20 years. Vacant since JEA relocated Apr 2023. Jun 2024 SV shows intact white tower with palm trees. Sold Feb 2026 to Live Oak Contracting for $1M. Plans for 180 mixed-income apartments ("The Jewel at 21 West") but renovation not started. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Richmond Generating Station (Philadelphia Electric Company). Massive coal-fired power plant on the Delaware River, designed by architect John T. Windrim. Built 1903-1928, abandoned since 1985. 839,556 sq ft. Jul 2025 SV shows fenced industrial complex with smokestacks, electrical substation in front, no activity. Chernow Development received $1M state grant for asbestos abatement but no construction started. Listed on National Register of Historic Places. Library of Congress HABS documentation. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Beury Building (formerly National Bank of North Philadelphia). 14-story Art Deco office tower at Broad St, Erie Ave, and Germantown Ave intersection. Built 1926, vacant approximately 50 years. Apr 2025 SV shows severely deteriorated facade, broken windows, boarded storefronts, graffiti. Developer Shift Capital (3701 North Broad LLC) completed $6M environmental remediation for planned Hotel Beury (Marriott), but key lender withdrew $23M loan Aug 2024. Redevelopment stalled indefinitely. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Germantown Town Hall. Historic city-owned civic building, approximately 28,000 rentable sq ft plus front rotunda. Vacant since 1998 — over 25 years. 34,400 sq ft total. Jun 2025 SV shows location at Germantown Ave intersection. Building partially obscured by mature trees and park. City has been unable to find developer willing to take on renovation. On Germantown Ave commercial corridor with multiple other blighted properties. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Germantown YWCA. Georgian Revival red-brick building designed by Louis H. Rush, built 1914. One of first racially integrated YWCAs in the country. Vacant since 1999 — over 25 years. Survived fires, demolition threats, and multiple failed redevelopment attempts. PRA sold to Germantown Settlement in 2006 (went bankrupt), then KBK Enterprises in 2016 (rights revoked after 5 years of no progress). Nov 2019 photosphere shows 3-story brick building with classical entrance, dark windows. Less than 1,000 ft from also-vacant Germantown Town Hall. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Packard Automotive Plant — former administration building. 112,000 sq ft, one of only two structures remaining after city-funded demolition of the 3.5M sq ft complex completed Dec 2024. The Packard Plant, built 1903 by Albert Kahn, was the world's largest abandoned factory. City unveiled "Packard Park" redevelopment plan Dec 2025 but Letter of Intent expired Mar 2026, future uncertain. Aug 2024 SV shows water tower and remaining industrial structures. Adjacent structure at 1539 E Grand Blvd (116,000 sq ft) also remains standing. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Plaza Tower. 45-story, 531-ft Brutalist office skyscraper, third tallest building in Louisiana. Built 1969. Vacant since 2002 due to mold and asbestos. Oct 2024 SV shows massive concrete tower standing at Howard and Loyola, across from Union Passenger Terminal. Sold Jun 2025 to Brian Gibbs and Lincoln Avenue Capital. Proposed $250M conversion to 300-unit senior assisted living, but city judge also cleared path for demolition Jan 2025. Future uncertain. One of tallest abandoned skyscrapers in the United States. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Charity Hospital (Big Charity). Massive Art Deco hospital building, approximately 1 million sq ft. Built 1939, one of largest hospitals in the South. Abandoned since Hurricane Katrina Aug 2005 — 20 years vacant. Tulane University plans $500M conversion to research center but project stalled. City pledged $20M Jul 2025, mayor vetoed, City Council overrode veto. Estimated redevelopment cost has more than doubled. Remains a defining New Orleans eyesore and preservation battleground. Link corrected Apr 2026.

address: 1101 S Flower St, Los Angeles, CA 90015
notes: Oceanwide Plaza ("Graffiti Towers"). Three unfinished mixed-use towers (residential + retail) across from Crypto.com Arena. Construction halted 2019 at 60% completion after Beijing-based Oceanwide Holdings cut funding. $1.2B already invested. Became infamous after massive graffiti tagging early 2024. Sep 2024 SV shows glass towers standing in downtown LA. Sale to KPC Group for $470M pending bankruptcy court approval as of Feb 2026. Tallest tower reaches 677 ft. Link corrected Apr 2026.
OPA exterior condition 7 (sealed/worst). Massive industrial warehouse with broken windows, peeling paint, AVAILABLE sign. Jul 2025 Street View.
OPA exterior condition 7. Massive deteriorating red brick industrial warehouse. May 2025 Street View.
OPA exterior condition 7. Large red brick industrial mill. Sep 2014 Street View (old imagery but building clearly derelict).
OPA exterior condition 7. Vacant 1-story commercial store with empty parking lot, overgrown edges. Aug 2024 Street View.
OPA exterior condition 7. 2-story masonry store completely sealed with metal security shutters. No business activity. Jul 2025 Street View.
OPA exterior condition 7. 2-story masonry store with sealed/bricked windows, adjacent vacant lot. Jul 2025 Street View.
OPA exterior condition 7. Light manufacturing building. Deteriorating brick, graffiti, overgrown vegetation. Jul 2025 Street View.
OPA exterior condition 7. 3-story masonry store/office/apartment in blighted Kensington corridor. Area shows widespread vacancy. Jul 2025.
OPA exterior condition 7. Industrial mill building. Blighted corridor with shuttered storefronts and vacant lots nearby. May 2025.

OPA exterior condition 6. 1-story masonry store/office.
OPA exterior condition 7. Industrial factory, masonry. Harrowgate industrial area.
OPA exterior condition 7. Industrial warehouse, masonry. Kensington industrial area.

address: 46 N B.B. King Blvd, Memphis, TN 38103
notes: Dermon Building. 10-story office building with distinctive glazed terra cotta facade (brown, yellow, green, white). Built 1925 by Dave Dermon, listed on National Register of Historic Places. Vacant since approximately 2010. 99,960 sq ft. Purchased Sep 2022 by Dermon Building OZ LLC for $1.5M. Downtown Memphis Commission approved plans Feb 2024 for 150-room Holiday Inn Express hotel conversion ($22.3M project) but construction never started — stalled post-COVID. Sep 2025 SV shows boarded ground-floor storefronts, no construction activity. Adjacent to Hotel Indigo on BB King Blvd. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Jefferson Plaza. 12-story mid-century office tower at Jefferson Ave and 2nd St, built 1954. 101,600 sq ft. Vacant 10+ years. Sold Dec 2025 for $3.1M to TCH Knoxville LLC (previously Trident Capital of America LLC for $2.8M). New roof installation underway but no construction or renovation of interior. May 2025 SV shows "Jefferson Plaza" signage, large murals painted on boarded ground-floor storefronts, dark empty upper-floor windows. Listed on Downtown Memphis Commission blighted buildings watchlist. Link corrected Apr 2026.

One AT&T Center (909 Chestnut). 44-story, 588-ft office tower on the Gateway Mall. 1,400,000 sq ft — Missouri's largest building by area. Built 1985. Vacant since 2017 when AT&T did not renew lease after employee count fell from 4,800 to 2,000. Sold Apr 2024 to Boston-based Goldman Group for $3.6M (previously valued at $205M). $350M mixed-use plan announced for 600+ apartments, observation deck, automated parking. Renovation put on hold May 2025 when state tax credit bill was tabled. Jan 2026 SV shows black barriers at ground level, no construction activity. One of tallest vacant buildings in the world. Link corrected Apr 2026.

1 Seaport / Seaport Residences / "Leaning Tower of FiDi." 60-story, 670-ft unfinished residential skyscraper in Financial District. 200,000 sq ft, 80 planned condo units. Construction stalled ~2020 after 3-inch northward lean detected, caused by soil improvement foundation method instead of deep pilings. Only half of windows installed. $250M invested. Developer Fortis Property Group (Jonathan Landau) subject to lawsuits; court-appointed receiver since 2021. Nov 2024 SV shows scaffolding and construction barriers, no active work. Standing incomplete for 5+ years. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Buffalo Grand Hotel (formerly Adams Mark, formerly Hilton). 486-room hotel, downtown Buffalo's largest. Opened late 1970s. Vacant since 2022 after 2021 arson fire gutted the ballroom area. Owner Harry Stinson has 18 code violations, $89K unpaid taxes/sewer fees. City of Buffalo filed legal action Jun 2025 to seize property due to neglect. Stinson received insurance payout Dec 2024 but no repairs made. Sep 2025 SV shows large multi-story hotel building standing vacant, no activity. Absence of hotel hampering city's convention business. Link corrected Apr 2026.

ExxonMobil Building (Humble Oil Building). 44-story, 1,200,000 sq ft International Style office tower designed by Welton Becket. Built 1962-63. Vacant since 2015 when ExxonMobil moved to Spring TX campus. Acquired 2023 by Bell Business Investments (Isaac Jacobowitz/Carnegie Management) for apartment conversion. Filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy Oct 2025 after conversion plans collapsed. Avoided foreclosure Dec 2025 with $16.1M payoff. Court filings describe water damage, broken windows, moldy drywall. Listed on National Register of Historic Places 2025. Interior photosphere shows gutted floors. Mar 2026 SV shows tower standing in downtown Houston. One of largest vacant buildings in Texas. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Alliance Bank Center. 16-story, 300,000 sq ft office tower at E 5th St and Cedar St in downtown St. Paul. Abruptly vacated Mar 2025 when owner Madison Equities sent tenants 2-day notice to evacuate, declaring inability to pay utilities. Subsequently vandalized — intruders broke in, emptied fire extinguishers, trashed floors. City limited downtown skyway access through building. St. Paul Downtown Development Corp purchased bank note Oct 2025. Building valued at less than a third of 2019 purchase price. Closed for the foreseeable future for safety. Aug 2024 SV shows tower from Cedar St side. Link corrected Apr 2026.
Abandoned multi-story brick apartment building with deteriorating ornate stone entrance, broken/missing windows. Apr 2024 SV. Near Buffington Park in downtown Gary.
Two-story brick building with broken/boarded windows, heavily deteriorated facade. Apr 2024 SV. One block south of 532 Pennsylvania.
Abandoned two-story wood-frame house, heavily overgrown with trees/brush, no signs of occupancy. Apr 2024 SV.
Large abandoned institutional building (possibly former school or parish hall) near St Monica & Luke Church. Partially collapsed roof, severe deterioration, broken windows throughout. Apr 2024 SV.
Low-rise commercial building on Broadway corridor. Area heavily blighted with overgrown vacant lots. Apr 2024 SV. Broadway is Gary's historic main commercial corridor.

2-story brick commercial/industrial building at E 21st Ave near Front St. Boarded and broken windows, heavy graffiti on side wall, overgrown lot. Clearly abandoned. Apr 2024 SV. In Gary's Midtown area near the Indiana Main Street-designated corridor (Broadway to Harrison, 21st to 27th Ave). Link corrected Apr 2026.

3-story brick commercial/industrial building on W 21st Ave. All windows boarded or bricked up, no signs of activity. Clearly abandoned. Apr 2024 SV. Larger building than nearby 200 E 21st. In Gary's Midtown area. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Deteriorated 2-story frame building at Adams St and W 21st Ave. Severely weathered wood siding, no signs of occupancy. Adjacent abandoned brick building to the south also vacant. Apr 2024 SV. In Gary's heavily blighted Midtown area. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Civic Theater, former movie theater built 1940s. On Mayor Duggan's M100 commercial blight list. Owner Dennis Kefallinos lost to tax foreclosure, daughter Dionysia bought back at auction 2024. City pursuing nuisance abatement. Oct 2023 SV shows large gray/brick building, no activity. Houston-Whittier Strategic Neighborhood Fund area.

Roberts Brass Manufacturing Company. On M100 list. Large abandoned industrial building with all windows boarded with red panels. Purchased 2006 for $392K by Southend Village Center LLC. Sporadic work observed but no OSHA compliance. Sep 2023 SV shows massive deteriorating factory from Junction Ave side.

Former River Plaza Apartments. On M100 list. Multi-story brick apartment building, went into foreclosure 2022. City targeted for demolition. Sep 2023 SV shows building still standing, construction activity on adjacent parcels.

Engine House #22, former firehouse built 1896. On M100 list. 119-year-old structure, only surviving firehouse of three designed in 1895. Later operated as Casa de Espana restaurant until closing. Sep 2025 SV partially obscured by truck.
Former Coverall Supply Co. On M100 commercial blight list. Kefallinos-owned property. City sued over blight conditions. Link corrected Apr 2026.
On M100 commercial blight list. Listed on LoopNet as flex property for sale. Kefallinos-associated. Southwest Detroit industrial area. Link corrected Apr 2026.
Former German social club. On M100 commercial blight list. Near Elmwood Cemetery in east side Detroit. Link corrected Apr 2026.

Large multi-story brick industrial/warehouse buildings flanking Railroad Ave. Overgrown vegetation between structures, no signage or activity. Jul 2024 SV. Near I-95 interchange in Bridgeport's industrial corridor.
Large multi-story industrial/warehouse building with sealed windows, loading dock area unused. Jul 2024 SV. Adjacent to 345 Railroad Ave complex, same industrial corridor near I-95.
Abandoned residential structure visible from Street View. Bridgeport east side neighborhood.

City Methodist Church. 9-story English Gothic church completed 1926, cost over $1M. Congregation peaked at 3,000. Closed 1975 due to declining attendance. Badly damaged by fire 1997. National Register of Historic Places. Also included Seaman Hall (community center), 1,000-seat theater, offices, dining hall, gymnasium. Google Maps interior photosphere shows spectacular ruin — collapsed roof, exposed Gothic arches, debris-filled nave. Gary's most iconic abandoned building. Proposed "ruin garden park" concept discussed but no construction. Apr 2024 SV. Link corrected Apr 2026.

United States Post Office. Massive Art Moderne style building at E 6th Ave and Massachusetts St. Built 1936 with federal Treasury Department funds as a New Deal project. Designed by architect Howard Lovewell Cheney. Closed 1970s. Added to National Register of Historic Places 1994 as part of Gary City Center Historic District. Apr 2024 SV shows large concrete/stone building with broken windows throughout, heavy graffiti, no signs of activity. One of the most significant abandoned federal buildings in the Midwest. Link corrected Apr 2026.

H. Gordon and Sons Department Store. 4-story Prairie School style building at 7th Ave and Broadway. Originally built 1923 as Elks lodge (designed by George W. Maher & Son), converted to department store 1934. Closed Sep 1972 due to declining downtown, crime. County welfare offices occupied 1973-unknown. Added to National Register of Historic Places 1994 (Gary City Center Historic District). Interior floors collapsed from water damage. Apr 2024 SV shows boarded storefronts, ornate entrance arch, broken upper windows.

4-story brick commercial building at NE corner of Broadway and 8th Ave. Broken and boarded windows, colorful murals painted on ground-floor storefronts. Part of continuous block of abandoned storefronts on east side of Broadway between 7th and 9th Ave. Apr 2024 SV. Adjacent to H. Gordon building (768 Broadway) to the north.

Former Sears, Roebuck & Company building. Massive 4-story Art Deco commercial building spanning Broadway from approximately 8th Ave south. Abandoned 1974. $6M renovation 1993 for Division of Family and Children offices — unclear if still in use. IU Northwest School of Arts mural project planned. Apr 2024 SV shows large facade with vertical pilasters and decorative cornices, all storefronts appear dark, no visible activity. Part of Gary City Center Historic District (National Register).

Palace Theater. 3,000-seat atmospheric theater designed by John Eberson, built 1925. Renaissance facade with ornate entrance and "PALACE" marquee. Closed 1972. Vacant 50+ years. $85M redevelopment plan announced 2024 by BWI LLC (The Palace Lofts — 251 housing units + retail) but delayed — developer unable to secure tax credits, no construction started as of 2026. National Register of Historic Places. Apr 2024 SV shows boarded storefronts, marquee intact.

Mahencha Apartments. 4-story Renaissance Revival apartment building with Spanish-inspired facade and asymmetrical tower, built 1928. Purchased by former Mayor Richard Hatcher 1978. Operations shut down 1984. $3.5M renovation announced 2001 but funding never materialized. Building in "development purgatory" — demolition considered more likely than restoration. Across from former Horace Mann High School on W 5th Ave (US-20). May 2024 SV from Arthur St shows building still standing, surrounded by vacant lots.

Knights of Columbus Building. 10-story brick building designed by Harry L. Porter and Ralph McNally (Cleveland), built 1925. Served as hotel, clubhouse, restaurant, and sport facility. National Register of Historic Places (1984), part of Gary City Center Historic District. Apr 2024 SV from Jefferson St shows distinctive stepped tower still standing, no visible signage or activity. Adjacent to Gary Public Library. Current occupancy unclear — building appears dark but not obviously boarded.

Horace Mann High School. Massive 3-story red brick classical school completed 1928. Housed up to 2,500 students. Featured gymnasium, theater, swimming pool. Closed summer 2004 due to declining enrollment. Sold to Gary Housing Authority 2021 for $5,000. Future uncertain — undergoing inspections. One of Indiana's largest abandoned schools. Across W 5th Ave from Mahencha Apartments. May 2024 SV from 5th Ave shows large building still standing behind vegetation.

3-story commercial building with ornate decorative terra cotta upper-floor facade. Ground floor storefronts covered/boarded, "2700" visible on building. Upper windows deteriorated. Adjacent to other vacant storefronts. Near Zeidman's Jewelry & Loan. Sep 2025 SV.

Row of 2-story brick commercial buildings. Multiple storefronts boarded up with plywood, upper windows dark or covered. Continuous block of vacancy along east side of Gratiot Ave between Chene St and Hale St. Sep 2025 SV.

2-story concrete/stucco commercial building at corner with traffic light. All upper windows boarded, ground floor sealed. Billboard on roof. Adjacent building to west partially demolished/deteriorated. Sep 2025 SV.

2-story brown/tan brick commercial building at corner. Ground floor storefronts sealed with roll-down gates or plywood. Upper windows blank/sealed. Vacant lot to the west. Active liquor store adjacent to the east. Sep 2025 SV.
Abandoned residential building visible from Street View. User-submitted.

Grande Ballroom. Legendary 1920s dance hall and 1960s-70s rock venue (MC5, Iggy Pop, Cream). Listed for sale at $500K in 2024. Still standing but severely deteriorated. National Register of Historic Places.
Vanity Ballroom. Art Deco 1929 dance hall in Jefferson-Chalmers Historic Business District. Aztec-themed interior. Closed 1960s, remains abandoned. National Register of Historic Places.
Ira Wilson & Sons Dairy building. Famous for giant cow head sculpture on roof. Former ice cream shop/dairy, long abandoned.
St. Agnes Church. Roofless abandoned church being reclaimed by nature. Originally established 1914 in LaSalle Park neighborhood. Iconic Detroit ruin.
Wohlfeil Furniture Company. Historic commercial building on Gratiot corridor. Artist Pat Perry painted mural on exterior 2017. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
St. Albertus Parochial School. Historic school building in east side Detroit. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
Deutsches Haus. Historic German cultural building on Mack Ave. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
Billinghurst Hotel. Historic hotel building near Midtown. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
Art Stove Company Office Building. Former industrial office building. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
The Apex Lounge. Former bar/lounge on Oakland Ave. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
Dexter Recreation and Market Center. Former commercial/recreation building on Dexter Ave. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
Bohemian National Hall. Historic Czech/Bohemian cultural hall in southwest Detroit. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
Dom Polski. Historic Polish community hall/cultural center. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
The Grosfield Building. Historic commercial building on Michigan Ave corridor. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
Hotel Yorba. Made famous by The White Stripes song. Historic hotel building. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
Danish Brotherhood Hall. Historic fraternal hall in Corktown area. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.
Stratford Theatre / Metropolitan Outreach Center. Former theater on West Vernor Highway in southwest Detroit. Listed by EHERG as needing renovation 2024.

Officially condemned. Multi-unit building (multiple unit numbers in condemnation records). Detroit Ave, Cudell neighborhood.

Starks Building. 14-story landmark office building at 4th St and Muhammad Ali Blvd. Built 1913, expanded 1923. Fully vacant since 2020 after last tenant Eddie Merlot's closed during protests/riots and did not reopen. $100M mixed-use redevelopment proposed by developer Jeff Underhill (264 affordable apartments, art studios, galleries) — seeking $18.6M from city Conservation Fund, construction not started. Louisville's 4th Street Live! entertainment district obscures SV coverage — building confirmed vacant via multiple news sources.

Kentucky Home Life Building. 20-story, 235-ft office tower at 5th and Jefferson, across from Louisville Metro Hall. Original 1912 structure (Brinton B. Davis, architect) expanded with 20-story addition. Possibly largest vacant structure in downtown Louisville. Last tenants left Feb 2022 citing lack of security/maintenance. Jul 2024 police raided building to remove trespassers who had been stealing wiring/metal, causing a major gas leak. Building undergoing foreclosure. Aug 2024 SV from S 5th St shows building.

Chambersburg Mall. Enclosed shopping mall near Scotland, PA off I-81 Exit 20. Opened 1982, peaked at 75+ stores in 1990s-2000s. Last tenant (Black Rose) closed Jun 30, 2023. Owned by Namdar Realty Group. Major roof leaks and collapsed ceilings reported — dangerous conditions requiring condemnation. Building still standing as of 2025 with no demolition or redevelopment plans. Oct 2015 SV from parking lot shows mall structure (SV predates closure).

Former AMF Walnut Hill Lanes bowling alley. 25,796 SF single-story commercial building on 2.21-acre parcel, built 1960. Located on S Crater Rd (US-301) near former Walnut Mall site (demolished 2006). Building vacant and for sale via Thalhimer. 131 surface parking spaces. Oct 2025 SV from S Crater Rd shows large vacant building with empty parking lot, no signage.

Former luggage factory complex. 255,000 SF across 13 buildings with classic factory features — wooden beams, masonry construction. Multi-story red brick industrial buildings. Acquired by Waukeshaw Development Nov 2021 for planned $45M conversion to 295 apartments. Construction was to begin early 2025 but no visible progress in Sep 2024 SV. Sep 2024 SV from W Wythe St shows massive vacant brick factory buildings, no construction activity.

Hawthorne Plaza Mall. Enclosed shopping mall on Hawthorne Blvd between 120th St and El Segundo Blvd. Opened 1977, last store closed 1999. Owned by Charles Company. Portions used for Tesla vehicle storage, HUSD admin offices, and police training center, but main mall structure sealed and abandoned 25+ years. Oct 2025 court injunction orders owner to redevelop or demolish by Aug 2026. Barbed wire/security fencing visible. Jul 2025 SV from Hawthorne Blvd shows sealed mall structure.

address: 2801 Pines Mall Dr, Pine Bluff, AR 71601
notes: Pines Mall. Enclosed shopping mall opened 1986, anchored by Dillards, JCPenney, Sears, Walmart. Closed permanently June 2020. Fire damaged food court 2022. Vandalism and squatting reported. Google Maps lists as "Permanently closed." Located off I-530 near E Harding Ave. Potential buyer (Mall Farm and Marketplace) announced plans in early 2026 but no construction started. Nov 2025 SV from AR-190 shows building set back from road.

address: 401 W Lancaster Ave, Fort Worth, TX 76102
notes: Texas & Pacific (T&P) Warehouse. 8-story, 580,000 SF Zigzag Moderne pale brick warehouse on 5-acre lot. Built 1931 as railroad freight warehouse, 611 ft long x 100 ft wide. Almost totally vacant since the 1970s — 50+ years abandoned. Bought by Cleopatra Investments 1998. Owner has restarted development efforts multiple times but no construction. 2023 city engineering study found $2M in needed repairs but building can be saved. Interior photospheres show destroyed interior with collapsed debris and graffiti. Jan 2025 SV from W Lancaster Ave shows massive boarded facade, no activity. Adjacent to Fort Worth Water Gardens and T&P Station.

Wonder Bread Factory. 5-story, 198,600 SF reinforced concrete industrial bakery with mushroom columns, built 1914. Operated by Interstate Bakeries, closed Oct 2004 when parent filed bankruptcy. Last 150 employees left. National Register of Historic Places. Purchased Sep 2020 by developer Harry Stinson (356 Fougeron Inc) for planned $30M apartment conversion, later changed to startup office incubator — neither project started. Mar 2025 report: roof covered in ice, each floor has inch+ of standing water, but concrete structure still very solid. Sep 2014 SV from 358 Fougeron shows cream/tan factory building. Building confirmed still standing Mar 2025.

Gwinnett Place Mall. 1.3 million SF enclosed regional mall on 39 acres. Opened 1984, peaked with 200+ stores. Last anchors (both Macy's stores) closed Mar 23, 2025. Gwinnett County Urban Redevelopment Agency purchased mall for $23M in 2021. County also bought former Sears anchor for $11.5M in Sep 2025, bringing total owned to 87.5 acres. Plans for demolition and "Global Villages" mixed-use redevelopment announced Feb 2023, but no construction started. Nov 2018 SV from parking lot shows mall building. Famous as filming location (Stranger Things Starcourt Mall).

address: 1600 S Peters St, New Orleans, LA 70130
notes: Market Street Power Plant. Historic defunct power plant complex, 160,000+ SF across 7 buildings on Mississippi River bank between Warehouse District and Lower Garden District. Built 1905 by New Orleans Railway & Light Co, expanded over decades. Last produced power 1973 — 53 years abandoned. Lauricella Land Co, Brian Gibbs Development, and Cypress Equities closed investment deal 2022. Planned Fall 2024 opening as entertainment/retail/hotel venue never materialized. May 2025 renovation put on hold pending state tax credits. No visible construction. Sep 2025 SV from S Peters St shows massive graffiti-covered brick industrial building with security fencing.

Metrocenter Mall. Mississippi's largest enclosed shopping mall, 1,250,000 SF on two levels with four anchor spaces. Opened Mar 1, 1978, near junction of I-20 and I-220/US-49. Last anchor (Sears) closed Jan 2012. Mall officially closed Aug 15, 2018. Last retail tenant (Burlington) left Feb 2022. City offices vacated 2024, making building completely empty. Ring road deteriorating, doors boarded with plywood. May 2025 Randy Travis and wife announced investment plans but no details or construction. May 2023 SV from ring road shows abandoned mall facade with standing water, broken windows, overgrown vegetation.

West Lake Mall (The Mall at Westlake). 323,000 SF single-level enclosed mall on 40-acre parcel north of Bessemer Superhighway, south of I-20/59. Built 1969 by Mel Simon. Had Sears, JCPenney, Grant's, Loveman's anchors. Last tenant (Bruno's) closed 2009 when chain filed bankruptcy — vacant 17 years. Auto dealer Anthony Underwood purchased for $950K in 2006. Range Light Investment Partners later purchased for $3M with plans for Marvel City Business Park (industrial/manufacturing suites), but no construction started as of Jun 2025. Oct 2018 SV shows vacant concrete-walled building with empty lot.

Terrence Building. 16-story, 160-ft brutalist reinforced concrete tower on Rochester Psychiatric Center campus. Opened 1959 as Rochester State Hospital psychiatric ward with 1,000 beds. Closed 1995. Vacant 31 years. Graffiti-covered, deteriorating, 6 elevators inoperable. 2019 arson fire. Developer Bob Morgan proposed $32M demolition and 110-room Hyatt House hotel 2015, city council approved demolition 2017, project stalled 2019. Estimated redevelopment cost risen to $32M+. Jul 2025 SV from south clearly shows massive abandoned tower looming over campus grounds.

Tulsa Promenade Mall (formerly Southroads Mall). 926,426 SF enclosed regional mall at 41st St and S Yale Ave. Opened 1974, renamed Tulsa Promenade 2007. Closed Sep 17, 2023 after owner Mike Kohan (Kohan Retail Investment Group) failed to address fire suppression and alarm code violations. City deemed mall "unfit for human occupancy." Jun 2025 storm ripped part of facade. $800K in delinquent property taxes (2021-2024), narrowly avoided auction May 2025. Interior trashed — unlocked doors, scattered trash, evidence of homelessness, fires reported. No redevelopment plans as of late 2025. Feb 2025 SV from 41st & Yale shows mall area.
Brown Marx Building. 16-story 1906 Chicago-style tower, 193K sq ft. NE corner 20th St & 1st Ave N. Part of 'Heaviest Corner on Earth'. One of Birmingham's most prominent abandoned skyscrapers.
Bush School (Ernest F. Bush Middle School). Built 1901 as Ensley School. Closed. Historic school in Ensley neighborhood.
Ensley High School. Founded 1901, current building 1908. Closed 2006, caught fire fall 2018. Historic school building in Ensley.
Bankhead Towers. Senior/disabled living high-rise, condemned Oct 2024 by City of Birmingham due to fire, building, and housing code violations. Residents evacuated.
Paramount Theatre (formerly Liberty Theatre). 1,700-seat theater designed by C. Howard Crane, opened 1918, closed 1976. Still standing. Plans for redevelopment.
Downtown office building. Mahoning County Land Bank involvement for remediation. Federal Street corridor.
Paterson Building. Historic downtown commercial building, condemned then partially collapsed into Brush Alley. Mott Foundation granted $3.5M to Communities First for renovation.
Commercial building on N Saginaw corridor. Genesee County Land Bank announced demolition plans.

address: 4256 Natural Bridge Ave, St. Louis, MO 63115
notes: Natural Bridge Ave commercial corridor in north St. Louis. Area documented with extensive commercial blight.

address: 4200 N Grand Blvd, St. Louis, MO 63107
notes: N Grand Blvd corridor in north St. Louis. Area with extensive vacancy and commercial blight.
N Broadway corridor in north St. Louis. Area documented by STL City Talk and Exploring St. Louis blogs for extensive blight.
Cass Ave corridor near NGA construction site. Jeff Vander Lou / St. Louis Place neighborhood. Area with extensive commercial vacancy.
Gary Union Station. Beaux-Arts railroad station built 1910, closed 1950s. Sits between two raised rail lines. National Register of Historic Places 2019. Indiana Landmarks 10 Most Endangered.
Genesis Convention Center. 7,000-seat arena built 1981. George Clinton, Public Enemy, Whitney Houston performed here. Closed 2020. City considering future plans as of 2025.

Centre City Building. 21-story reinforced concrete skyscraper built 1904, once Dayton's tallest. Originally United Brethren HQ, Wright Brothers connection (father Milton was bishop). Chicago Commercial style. Long vacant. Renovation plans for 200 apartments announced.
Central Motors Building. Built 1926 as Oldsmobile dealership. Outside Wright Dunbar Historic Business District. Owned by Wright Dunbar Inc. since 2010. Vacant commercial building.

Belvedere Manor Apartments. Condemned by City of Jackson, residents ordered to vacate Mar 1 2025, demolition ordered Mar 13 2025. Demolition not yet completed as of Mar 2026. Squatters and illegal dumping reported. Near Key Elementary. Est. $500K to demolish.

Camden Carnegie Library (Camden Free Public Library). Neo-Classical Revival, built 1905, funded by Andrew Carnegie. Abandoned 1986, roof collapsed, trees growing inside. National Register of Historic Places 1992. NJ Most Endangered. Conversion plans announced 2018 but stalled.

Sterick Building. Gothic Revival 31-story skyscraper, 365 ft, built 1929. Tallest in American South until 1957. Vacant since 1986. NE corner Third and Madison. Purchased 2023 by Constellation Properties, $300K TN Historic grant Apr 2024. Renovation planned but not yet started.
Dermon Building. 10-story Renaissance-detailed building, built 1925 for $800K. Colorful glazed terra cotta facade. National Register of Historic Places. Largely/completely vacant since 2010.

Firestone Plant 1 (Firestone Tire & Rubber HQ). Built 1910, iconic clock tower. Vacant 10+ years, city-owned. High asbestos contamination. City seeking partial demolition, preserving facade and clock tower. Ohio Endangered Historic Sites list.

Springfield Isolation Hospital. 5-story Art Deco, built 1930-31 by Kirkham & Parlett for tuberculosis treatment. Largest Art Deco building in Springfield. Unused for decades. Preservation Massachusetts 2022 Most Endangered. City acquired 17-acre campus for $1 from Vibra Healthcare.

Central Warehouse. Massive cold/dry storage facility built 1927. Albany's 'worst eyesore.' Mayor declared state of emergency Jan 2024 due to structural failure. Developer abandoned renovation plans. $60M Restore NY project initiated for demolition/remediation. Awarded $9.75M Round 1 Restore NY.

Saenger Theatre. Designed by Emile Weil, opened Nov 17 1924. 'Showplace of the South.' Closed 1975. Water damage and vandalism. National Register 1995. City council approved roof/plumbing renovation 2023. SE corner W 2nd Ave & Pine St.

address: 440 S Main St, Pine Bluff, AR 71601
notes: Hotel Pines. Designed by George R. Mann (architect of Arkansas State Capitol). Center of Pine Bluff social life for 50+ years. Closed 1970 when passenger rail ended. National Register 1979. NW corner Main & W 5th Ave.
National Guard Armory. Built 1931-32. Listed on National Register of Historic Places. Former military facility in downtown Pine Bluff. Part of Downtown Pine Bluff Historic Commercial District.
